title: Plastic waste generation by region and polymer
subtitle: Polymer types are as follows: Acrylonitrile Butadiene (AB), Acrylonitrile Styrene Acrylate (ASA), Styrene Acrylonitrile (SAN), Polyurethane (PUR), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polystyrene (PS), Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE).
caption:
Plastic waste generation by region and polymer, 2019

Polymer types are as follows: Acrylonitrile Butadiene (AB), Acrylonitrile Styrene Acrylate (ASA), Styrene Acrylonitrile (SAN), Polyurethane (PUR), Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC), Polystyrene (PS), Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE), Polypropylene (PP), Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET), High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE).

This stacked bar chart displays the regional composition of plastic waste generation for thirteen distinct polymer categories in 2019, with each horizontal bar representing 100% of the global waste for that specific material type. The data segments the total volume by geographic origin, using color-coded bands to distinguish contributions from Europe, China, Asia (excluding China and India), the Middle East & North Africa, India, Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas (excluding the USA), Oceania, the United States, and a category for road marking coatings. Across the majority of polymer types, the United States, represented by the teal segment on the far right, consistently accounts for the largest single share of waste generation, typically occupying the space between the 80% and 100% marks on the horizontal axis. China, shown in maroon, and Europe, shown in blue, form the next most substantial blocks, generally appearing as the second and third largest contributors respectively within most categories such as PVC, PUR, and PS. The green band representing Asia (excluding China and India) adds a moderate layer to the total, while regions like Oceania and Sub-Saharan Africa appear as very thin slivers, indicating minimal contribution to the global totals for these specific polymers. The chart includes a category for road marking coatings which follows the same regional distribution pattern as the consumer plastics. The visual arrangement highlights that a small number of regions dominate the generation of waste across all listed polymer types, with the United States, China, and Europe comprising the bulk of the volume for materials ranging from fibres and elastomers to bioplastics and HDPE. The distribution of waste generation remains heavily concentrated in these three specific geographic areas regardless of the polymer classification.
